Review

Negative impacts of climate change on crop yields are underestimated
============================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Rogério de S.Nóia Júnior 1, Senthold Asseng 2, Christoph Müller 3, Jean-Charles Deswarte 4, Jean-Pierre Cohan 5, Pierre Martre 1

Highlights
----------

*   Crop models are widely used to assess climate change impacts on crop production. 
*   Recent weather extremes have exposed limitations in models’ ability to capture complex crop × climate interactions. 
*   Increasing attention has turned to modeling multiple, interacting stressors such as extreme drought, heat, and excessive rainfall. 
*   Advances in data availability and computational methods, including machine learning, are opening new opportunities for improving crop growth models and crop yield projections under climate extremes. 

Abstract
--------

Crop simulation models are routinely used to project the impacts of climate change on crop yields. However, such models perform poorly when simulating extreme historical events. We reviewed current crop models according to the processes they simulate. The review suggests the inability of most models to simulate several mechanisms of adverse climatic impacts on crops, such as those caused by heavy rain and waterlogging. Current crop models are therefore likely to increasingly underestimate climate impacts on crops if adverse climate conditions escalate in frequency and severity as expected. Improved modeling is crucial to accurately project crop yields and enhance the resilience of global food systems under extreme weather.

---------------------------------------

Limitations of process-based crop models in capturing extreme weather impacts
-----------------------------------------------------------------------------

--- SOURCE 3: The impact of weather patterns on inter-annual crop yield variability ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388

SUMMARY:
The MO30 classification has been applied in various contexts, including medium to long-range weather forecasting (Neal et al., 2024), fluvial and coastal flooding (Hendry et al., 2019; Neal et al., 2018; Schillereff et al., 2019), drought analysis and prediction (Richardson et al., 2018, Richardson et al., 2020a, Richardson et al., 2020b), characterisation of precipitation (Fereday et al., 2018; Richardson et al., 2020a, Richardson et al., 2020b), temperature analysis (Kendon et al., 2020), forecasting lightning activity (Souto et al., 2023, Hayward et al., 2022; Wilkinson and Neal, 2021), marine operations and forecasts (Steele et al., 2017, Steele et al., 2018, Steele et al., 2022), future climate change impacts (Cotterill et al., 2023; Perks et al., 2023; Pope et al., 2022), volcanic ash disrupting UK air space (Harrison et al., 2022), weather-induced power outages in the national grid (Souto et al., 2024), business performance (Brown et al., 2019), and temperature-related excess mortality (Huang et al., 2020). Huntingford, R.F. Pywell, J.W. Redhead, E.J. Kendon Resilience of UK Crop Yields to Changing Climate Extremes   (2021), 10.5194/esd-2021-92 Google Scholar

Highlights
----------

*   •Weather patterns exhibit a complex relationship with interannual variations in UK Winter Wheat yields. 
*   •Northern UK shows differing relationship between weather patterns and yield compared to East and Southeast England. 
*   •Future predicted weather pattern changes suggest that soils will need to store moisture to prevent yield reduction. 

Abstract
--------

Inter-annual variations in crop production have significant implications for global food security, economic stability, and environmental sustainability. Existing crop yield prediction models primarily using meteorological variables may not adequately encapsulate the full breadth of weather influences on crop development processes, such as compound or extreme events. Incorporating weather patterns into crop models could provide a more comprehensive understanding of the environmental conditions affecting growth, enabling more accurate and earlier yield predictions.

Our study examines 30 distinct UK Met Office weather patterns (MO30) based on mean sea level pressure. We investigate their association with weather conditions that limit winter wheat yield in the UK (1990–2020). Blocked, negative North Atlantic Oscillation (NAO) patterns create the highest risk of temperatures that are below optimal for crop yield. However, the connection between weather patterns and yield is complex, with differing effects at a regional scale and even at which point in the growth cycle they appear. It was found that anticyclonic weather patterns during sowing, emergence, vernalisation, anthesis, and grain filling exhibit a relationship with good crop yields with a Spearman correlation coefficient of up to 0.55 for a single weather pattern (WP3 during vernalisation in South East England), whilst cyclonic patterns can help during the terminal spikelet phenological phase. The strongest positive correlations were during sowing, emergence, and vernalisation, whilst the largest negatives were observed in anthesis and grain filling.

The potential of combining weather patterns with existing crop simulation models to produce earlier and more accurate yield predictions is shown. This would enable effective crop management and climate mitigation strategies, critical to strengthening food security. Projected changes in weather pattern occurrences in the late 21st century will likely reduce crop yields. This is due to increased cyclonic weather patterns, which bring warmer, wetter conditions during the wheat's vernalisation stage, followed by warmer, drier conditions during the anthesis and grain-filling phases.

1. Introduction
---------------

The world's agricultural landscape is dynamic and complex, with interactions between various meteorological, geographical, and agronomic factors that collectively influence crop yields ([Lesk et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0230); [Lobell et al., 2011](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0235)). Inter-annual variations in crop production have significant implications for global food security, economic stability, and environmental sustainability ([Kang et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0195)). Understanding the underlying drivers of these fluctuations is a critical endeavour, particularly in an era marked by an accelerated change in climate patterns and a growing global population ([Alexandratos and Bruinsma, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0010)). Among these drivers are atmospheric circulation patterns, which play a crucial role in shaping weather conditions that affect agricultural productivity.

Circulation types represent specific configurations of atmospheric patterns, often associated with recurring weather conditions. In this paper, circulation types encompass Weather Patterns (WP) and Weather Regimes. A WP is characterised by the persistence of a specific atmospheric circulation system over a defined geographic area, typically for a duration of approximately one to a few days, as noted by various research ([James, 2007](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0180); [Lamb, 1972](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0225); [Neal et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0270)). In contrast, WRs are distinguished by a smaller set of patterns with longer duration, often extending up to a week ([Fereday et al., 2008](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0095); [Ferranti et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0105); [Vautard, 1990](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0440)). While alternative terminology and definitions exist, this paper adopts these terms and their associated meanings for clarity.

In this paper, we use a classification of WP developed by the UK Met Office known as MO30 ([Neal et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0270)). [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#f0005) provides a visual representation of the MO30 and offers a detailed description of each WP. The patterns were derived by applying a clustering algorithm to the European and North Atlantic Daily to Multi-Decadal Climate Variability (EMULATE, [Ansell et al. (2006)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0020)) dataset. EMULATE provides a gridded daily historical Mean Sea Level Pressure (MSLP) anomaly dataset for a North Atlantic–European domain (30°W–20°E, 35°–70°N) at a 5° vertical and horizontal resolution spanning from 1850 to 2003. The MO30 classification has been applied in various contexts, including medium to long-range weather forecasting ([Neal et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0280)), fluvial and coastal flooding ([Hendry et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0150); [Neal et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0275); [Schillereff et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0360)), drought analysis and prediction ([Richardson et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0335), [Richardson et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0345), [Richardson et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0350)), characterisation of precipitation ([Fereday et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0100); [Richardson et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0345), [Richardson et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0350)), temperature analysis ([Kendon et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0205)), forecasting lightning activity ([Souto et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0380), [Hayward et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0140); [Wilkinson and Neal, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0445)), marine operations and forecasts ([Steele et al., 2017](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0395), [Steele et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0400), [Steele et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0405)), future climate change impacts ([Cotterill et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0060); [Perks et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0295); [Pope et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0305)), volcanic ash disrupting UK air space ([Harrison et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0135)), weather-induced power outages in the national grid ([Souto et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0385)), business performance ([Brown et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0035)), and temperature-related excess mortality ([Huang et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0175)).

Crop yields are highly sensitive to temperatures outside the optimal range. Excessive high and low temperatures can reduce the production of dry matter and, in extreme cases, even halt growth entirely ([Grace, 1988](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0125)). Plants react more to absolute temperature changes than relative ones, showing distinct threshold responses to temperature. This differs from their response to water deficiency, where water uptake and dry matter production have a continuous and resilient connection across a broad spectrum of water availability ([Tanner and Sinclair, 1983](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0415)). Excessive rainfall can significantly reduce crop yields, similar to the effects of extreme drought. This is due to waterlogging, poor soil drainage, and increased soil water storage, which negatively affect plant growth and yield ([Li et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb2010); [Slater et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0375)).

Most of the commonly used crop simulation models, such as AquaCrop ([Steduto et al., 2009](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0390)), APSIM ([Keating et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0200)), SIRIUS ([Jamieson et al., 1998](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0185)), Yield-SAFE ([Van Der Werf et al., 2006](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0430)), CERESWheat ([Bannayan et al., 2003](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0030)) and WheatGrow-PROSAIL (L. [Zhang et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0460)), use precipitation, air temperature, and/or solar radiance to represent environmental conditions. While these models have been very successful in simulating and forecasting yield, they have limitations due to data availability ([Corcoran et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0055)). Weather can account for 80% of crop yield variability ([Hoogenboom, 2000](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0165)), but these models do not consider how weather also affects interconnected processes such as pollinator activity and yield ([Aldercotte et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0005); [Kammerer et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0190); [Reilly et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0330)). Using WPs can help generalise the environment rather than focusing on specific meteorological variables such as temperature, humidity, and precipitation. Given the increased accuracy of models forecasting WPs at the medium to long range (beyond five days) ([Büeler et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0040); [Ferranti et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0105); [Matsueda and Palmer, 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0250); [Neal et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0270), [Neal et al., 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0280)), integrating them into crop growth simulation models could produce earlier predictions and improve accuracy.

There is a significant research gap in understanding the influence of WPs on crop yields, particularly regarding inter-annual variations in UK winter wheat. The present study aims to investigate the relationship between WPs and interannual crop yield variability, ultimately informing agricultural decision-making, resource management, and policy development by providing earlier and more accurate yield forecasts. The specific objectives are to 1) identify which WPs are associated with yield-limiting weather conditions, 2) investigate the direct relationship between WPs and crop yield across various winter wheat growth stages and regions, and 3) evaluate the implications of climate change on crop yields through future changes in WP occurrence.

2. Data and methods
-------------------

### 2.1. UK wheat yield data

National and regional Winter Wheat (WW) yield data were obtained from the UK Department for Environment, Food and Rural Affairs ([Defra, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0075)) for the three main EU Nomenclature of Territorial Units for Statistics (NUTS) ([European Union, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0080)) growing regions in the UK (now referred to as International Territorial Levels (ITLs) in the UK). The geographic focus was on these central wheat production regions, which are (i) Northeastern Scotland, Eastern Scotland, and the Northeast England region (Northern, SNE); (ii) East Midlands, Yorkshire, and the Humber regions (Midlands, EMYH); and (iii) Southeast and Eastern region (Southern, SEE) ([Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#f0010)). All are level one NUTS/ITL regions except Northeastern Scotland and Eastern Scotland, which are level two. Combined, these areas account for over 80% of wheat production in the UK ([Defra, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0075)). This Defra dataset is part of an annual survey of the UK agricultural industry. It is taken from the England Cereals and Oilseeds Production Survey and Scotland Cereal Production and Disposal Survey. National data starts in 1885, while regional data begins in 1990.

Fig. 2. The three areas of interest for this study. They are formed of the Nuts ([European Union, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0080)) regions: Northern, SNE (blue): “North Eastern Scotland”, “Eastern Scotland”, “Southern Scotland”, and “North East (England)”, Midlands, EMYH (yellow): “Yorkshire” and “East Midlands (England)”, and Southern SEE (green): “East of England”, “South East (England)”.

Measuring and contrasting the impact of WPs on annual WW yields over decades and across regions is a complex task due to the multitude of factors influencing both short (each season) and long-term (5–10 years) crop yield. The long-term factors encompass advancements in science and technology, such as plant genetics, fertilisers, pesticides, and irrigation systems, and short-term effects, such as meteorological conditions (highlighted in Supplementary Fig. 1). As a result, long-term WW yield data is non-stationary, characterised by varying means and standard deviations over long periods. To effectively model and visually represent the impact of WPs on agriculture, it is crucial to remove the long-term trends. [Lu et al. (2017)](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0240) reviewed several methods for detrending yield data, identifying the locally weighted smoothing curve (LOWESS) as one of the most effective approaches. LOWESS fits a smooth, locally weighted regression line through the data points, effectively capturing the underlying trend while minimising the impact of short-term fluctuations and outliers. By fitting this smooth curve to the yield data, LOWESS estimated the trend component. The yield values are divided by this estimated trend line to remove the long-term variations. This detrended data reveals the underlying seasonal and residual variations (yield anomalies).

We report the Spearmen correlation coefficient to determine whether there is a connection between anomalous WP occurrences and anomalous WW yields. Using the Northern, Midlands, and Southern regions, we compare the results of national and regional datasets.

### 2.2. Meteorological data

Daily minimum (T min) and maximum (T max) temperature data, as well as daily total precipitation data, were obtained from the HadUK-Grid 1 km observational dataset provided by the UK Met Office through the Centre for Environmental Data Analysis ([Hollis et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb2015)). This gridded dataset was used to calculate regional averages of the meteorological variables for our areas of interest, Northern, Midlands, and Southern.

### 2.3. Weather pattern occurrence data

A daily historical MO30 WP classification dataset ([Neal, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0265)) was used. This dataset is based on daily MSLP from the ERA5 reanalysis ([Hersbach, 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0155)). The process involves converting 1200 UTC ERA5 MSLP fields to anomalies, using a smoothed ERA5 daily climatology for the same period. The anomalies are then assigned to the closest matching WP definition from MO30 ([Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#f0005)), also defined by its MSLP anomalies. The assignment method is determined by pairing with the smallest grid point average sum of squared differences ([Harrison et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0135); [Neal et al., 2016](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0270)).

### 2.4. Connecting WP to crop-limiting weather conditions and yield

While many studies linking weather patterns to extreme weather events focus on meteorological definitions (e.g., emphasising the magnitude and rarity of events like annual maxima or events exceeding thresholds) ([Neal et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0275); [Richardson et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0335), [Richardson et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0345), [Richardson et al., 2020b](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0350)), this study adopts an agricultural perspective. We define extreme temperatures based on their direct impact on crops rather than solely on their statistical frequency. Crop sensitivity to weather conditions varies across phenological stages ([Porter and Gawith, 1999](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#bb0310)). Therefore, we divide the crop calendar year into four key phases: sowing to emergence (Planting), which is hereafter termed the planting stage for simplicity (the initial growth phase when seeds germinate and seedlings emerge); Vernalization (Growth), referred to as the establishing stage (a cold period crucial for the plant's development and flowering); Terminal Spikelet (Heading), termed the heading stage (when the plant develops its grain-bearing structures); and Anthesis and Grain-filling (Filling), referred to as the filling Stage (the final phase when the plant flowers and produces grains). These stages' dates and associated optimal minimum and maximum temperatures are summarised in [Table 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0048969724073388#t0005). To identify weather patterns associated with exceeding crop-specific extreme temperature thresholds, we analysed T min, T max, and WP anomalies for key phenological stages over the period from 1990 to 2020. We identified dates when T min and T max exceeded extreme temperature thresholds for WW and recorded the corresponding weather patterns. Finally, we calculated the percentage of each WP's total occurrence during each phenological stage that coincided with extreme temperature events.

# Forecasting crop yields in an era of extreme weather

## 

![A farmer holds some of the strawberries they have picked from their farm.](https://staticcdn.mbzuai.ac.ae/mbzuaiwpprd01/2025/01/Strawberries-fruit-environment-sustainability-climate-1.jpg)

Over the course of his career, [Fakhri Karray](https://mbzuai.ac.ae/study/faculty/professor-fakhreddine-fakhri-karray/), Professor of Machine Learning at MBZUAI, has worked to design intelligent machines and systems that can benefit society. These technologies have spanned many fields, including transportation, manufacturing, healthcare and agriculture.

Karray, who served as the Loblaws research chair in AI at the University of Waterloo in Canada, and was a founding co-director of the university’s AI Institute before joining MBZUAI, has been particularly interested in pursuing projects where tools of artificial intelligence and machine learning can be used to promote sustainability.

Along with colleagues from the University of Waterloo, he is co-author of a study that explores how historical climate and agricultural data can be used to help forecast crop yields. The research focuses specifically on the relationship between extreme temperatures and the impact they have on strawberry yields in an area of California that is the most prolific strawberry-producing region in the world.

Extreme weather is becoming more frequent across the globe due to climate change. In 2023, for example, unusually [cold and wet weather in California](https://www.latimes.com/business/story/2023-06-19/what-happened-strawberries-spring-weather) resulted in a $100 million loss to the strawberry industry that had far reaching implications. Similar patterns occurred around the world during that year with global losses of more than $600 million. Better predictions about a harvest may have benefits for consumers, farmers and the wider agricultural industry.

What’s more, Karray and his colleagues note that their findings have the potential to help scientists gain a better understanding of the vulnerabilities of agricultural systems in an era of climate change that has been marked by extreme weather across the globe.

## **Pricing a berry**

There are many factors that influence the price consumers pay for fruits and vegetables and pricing is a complex activity shaped by different actors and market conditions. Retailers often negotiate prices to purchase crops from producers well before the crop is even planted. This negotiated price is based on past prices and forecasts for the coming growing season that relate to the amount of crop farmers will plant and expectations for the weather. Sometimes, of course, these forecasts turn out to be wrong.

If the yield of a crop in a growing season turns out to be significantly below what is expected, retailers need to increase the price consumers pay so that they can cover their own costs. “In some recent years, the industry has been unable to accurately forecast the price of the crop before the seeds are planted,” Karray says. “This has affected the whole supply chain, from the growers to the retailers to the consumers, and prices have at times gotten out of control.”

## **Climate’s impact on prices**

Over the years, scientists have developed several methods for modeling the relationship between climate and crop yield. These approaches have tended to work well for specific crops grown in certain regions. They are, however, less accurate when they must account for extreme fluctuations in the weather that are increasing in frequency with global climate change.

Karray explains that there is therefore the need for new models “that can account for uncertainty and variability, offering a more accurate approach for and risk assessment”. With this study, Karray and his colleagues are seeking to improve what is known as “extreme value prediction” and they do this by building a statistical model that can incorporate the uncertainties and interdependencies between weather extremes and yield.

Strawberries are good crops for this kind of analysis. The plants are sensitive to variations in temperature, as the variations affect the way the plants process nutrients and carry out photosynthesis. High temperatures can slow plants’ development and reduce the quality of the fruit they produce.

## **The right data for analysis**

Karray and his team analyzed data collected between 2011 and 2019 by growers in the Santa Maria Valley, a major agricultural region approximately 150 miles north of Los Angeles that produces 80% of the strawberries grown in the U.S. The researchers also analyzed temperature data for this period that was gathered by the State of California. “We have relied on a group of entities to provide us with these datasets,” Karray says. “Without one of them, we might not have had adequate data for this kind of forecasting.”

Growers in the area use a technique called drip irrigation to water their plants. The technique is in large part standardized across the region, so the researchers didn’t account for precipitation in their model. Past studies have also shown that the correlation between crop yield and precipitation is less strong than is the correlation between yield and temperature.

Karray and his colleagues employed what is known as a multivariate probabilistic analysis of the data, which is a method used to predict outcomes where multiple variables are at play and there is uncertainty about how the variables influence each other. They also used a statistical technique called a vine copula, which is useful in illuminating dependencies between variables. (In this case, the variables were monthly maximum strawberry yield, monthly minimum strawberry yield and monthly temperature anomaly.) The technique gets its name because it “couples” multivariate joint distributions to the individual variables’ distributions. Copulas have often been used in other fields, including finance and economics.

In addition to the multivariate analysis, the researchers conducted univariate analyses, which focused only on the distributions of individual variables. These, however, tended to underestimate the likelihood of events that led to crop loss and overlooked “the intricate relationship between temperature anomalies and crop yields,” the researchers explained in the study.

Multivariate copula analysis, on the other hand, allowed for a more accurate assessment of the conditional probabilities of the variables and illuminated the “joint dependence structure between variables.” The researchers found that a temperature anomaly of more than 3 degrees Fahrenheit was likely to result in a reduction in yield.

While their findings provide insight into the relationship between extreme temperatures and strawberry crop yield, the researchers note that any kind of extrapolation of these findings to other regions or crops should be done with caution. They also write that they would like to study similar data on a longer timescale, which might provide more insights into how temperature extremes and their effects are changing over time.

## **Global challenge, global solution**

A better understanding of the way extreme temperatures affect crop yields may help farmers and the agricultural industry prepare for a world in which the climate is ever more volatile. That said, addressing climate change is a monumental task.

Karray believes that while scientists can develop artificial intelligence systems that help to reduce the impacts of climate change, comprehensive solutions will require the cooperation of governments, businesses and society at large. “The planet is under huge pressure today and changes in the weather are going to affect everyone on the planet,” he says.

And even if the increase in global temperatures can’t be reversed or arrested, Karray notes that some of the most catastrophic impacts from climate change could be lessened if we are able to mitigate the severity of weather extremes and anomalies that have become all too common in recent years.

For more information about this study or related work, [please reach out to Prof. Karray](https://mbzuai.ac.ae/study/faculty/professor-fakhreddine-fakhri-karray/).

# Crop Weather Forecast: Powerful Analysis for Impacts on Yield

## Crop Weather Forecasting: Enabling Analysis for Grain Trading

CropProphet features a crop weather forecast that we call the “weather outlook yield forecast.” For this feature, the weather forecast models are used to drive the yield forecasts. The benefit to CropProphet customers is gaining an understanding of the risk of the change in the yield forecast 15 to 30 days into the future. This will help to manage corn and soybean futures price risks.

A new enhancement recently released allows [CropProphet Enterprise](https://www.cropprophet.com/yield-forecast-system/) users to see the best case and worst case crop yield and production forecast as implied by several ensemble weather forecast models. Our methodology to produce this important analysis is described below.

![CropProphet best/worst case corn production forecast](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![CropProphet best/worst case corn production forecast](https://www.cropprophet.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/crop_weather_forecast_ECMWF_best_worst_case_corn_yield.png)

## Crop Yield Modeling

CropProphet provides weather-driven [crop yield forecasts](https://www.cropprophet.com/corn-yield-forecast-model/) focused on corn, soybeans, and winter wheat. The product, however, is much more than just a weather forecast for the corn belt. CropProphet is based on a sophisticated, machine learning-based model that relates daily weather conditions to crop yields and production.

The model itself is relatively simple. It’s based on this formula.

![Corn yield forecasting equation](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20889%20128'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Corn yield forecasting equation](https://www.cropprophet.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/yieldmodel.png)

The yield in any year for corn, soybeans, and winter wheat is considered to be the combination of technology trends + the impacts of weather + the error. This equation, however, allows CropProphet to model yield based not only on what has happened with the weather but also based on weather forecast information. This capability is called the “weather outlook” component of the CropProphet crop yield forecasts.

## CropProphet Crop Weather Forecast

The crop weather forecast used in the CropProphet’s weather outlook component are obtained from several independent weather forecast models: NOAA’s 15-day [Global Forecast System](https://www.ncdc.noaa.gov/data-access/model-data/model-datasets/global-forcast-system-gfs) (GFS) and 30-day [Climate Forecast System](https://cfs.ncep.noaa.gov/) (CFSv2), and the world-leading 15-day E[uropean Centre for Medium-Range Forecasts](https://www.ecmwf.int) (ECMWF) weather forecast model. Each of these models is an ensemble modeling system, which means that each model provides a collection of “ensemble member” forecasts that are equally likely but often very different from each other. Most weather forecast maps that show ECMWF data, for example, are the average of the ensemble, as shown below.

![Crop Weather Forecast: Example degree day forecast](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20809%20542'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![Crop Weather Forecast: Example degree day forecast](https://www.cropprophet.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/ECWMF_GDD_Forecast.png)

Example ECMWF average ensemble ag weather forecast: Growing Degree Days

For example, the ECMWF crop weather forecast includes 51 members, and these 51 forecasts start to become different quickly after about a week. The CropProphet yield and production models are applied to each of the ensemble members in turn, leading to an ensemble of yield and production forecasts. It is also important to note that each weather forecast model (GFS, CFSv2, ECMWF) has its own systematic biases relative to the [PRISM](http://www.prism.oregonstate.edu/) and [ERA5](https://cds.climate.copernicus.eu/) data that CropProphet relies on, and therefore the first step in the weather outlook process is to perform a bias correction for each county and each weather variable.

![51 Corn YIeld Forecasts generated by the ECMWF weather forecast model](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%201024%20572'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![51 Corn YIeld Forecasts generated by the ECMWF weather forecast model](https://www.cropprophet.com/wp-content/uploads/2020/05/yield51-1024x572.png)

51 realizations of an ECMWF based weather outlook corn yield forecast

After bias correction, each of the ensemble member forecasts is used to generate a county-level crop weather forecast. These county-level forecasts are production weighted to calculate the national yield forecast. The median yield forecast of the 51 possible forecasts is considered to be the most likely case; this provides the “expected 15/30-day change” for the CropProphet interface (as in the example above).

The weather outlook component has been upgraded for 2022 by implementing a number of behind-the-scenes computational refinements, and also by adding “worst-case” and “best-case” map options to the website interface.

![CropProphet best/worst case corn production forecast](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20171'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![CropProphet best/worst case corn production forecast](https://www.cropprophet.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/crop_weather_forecast_ECMWF_best_worst_case_corn_yield.png)

Weather Outlook Best Case/Worst Case yield forecast change example

In the examples above, the difference between the worst-case and best-case ECMWF outlooks for corn production is almost 6 bu/ac, and this represents a plausible range of uncertainty for how the corn production forecast will change over the next 14 days. The two extremes are determined by finding the minimum and maximum **national aggregated** yield among the ensemble members and then creating county-level maps of the forecast changes caused by those two members.

It is important to note that these maps do not represent the worst-case and best-case at each location separately because such maps would be unrealistic (i.e. no weather outcome will degrade or improve yields at all locations simultaneously). Instead, this view represents two plausible weather scenarios from the weather forecast model that result in the best and worst-case national yield and production outcomes.

![New call-to-action](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%200%200'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![New call-to-action](https://no-cache.hubspot.com/cta/default/6948257/3b5cc4bd-f143-4e26-8a00-db4ea4dab649.png)

## Crop Weather Forecast: Ensemble Forecast

We can provide even more information about the impact of the crop weather forecast on the future corn yield forecast.

![](data:image/svg+xml,%3Csvg%20xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'%20viewBox='0%200%20500%20292'%3E%3C/svg%3E)
![](https://www.cropprophet.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/07/ECMWF_corn_yield_forecst_distribution.png)

The 51 Member ECMWF Ensemble Weather Forecast corn yield forecast change distribution.

The above graphic shows the impact of each member of the ECMWF weather forecast.  The ECMWF weather forecast is technically 51 equally likely possible weather forecasts.  We apply the CropProphet yield forecast model to each of the 51 forecasts.  This creates 51 different yield forecasts which we can compare the the “year-to-date” yield forecast.  In other words, we can look at how the ECMWF forecast suggests the corn yield forecast might change over the next 14 days.

As the season advances, CropProphet’s weather outlook component will provide valuable advance warning of likely future crop weather impacts, and the new worst-case and best-case options will add a useful quantitative range of uncertainty surrounding these impacts. As always, all of the data displayed on the website interface is available for download on our SFTP server, and interested customers should inquire about Modeler options that allow for the backtesting of automated trading strategies.
